questions.question: An outbreak of hemorrhagic colitis occurs in a population of C57BL/6 mice exposed to Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (EHEC). Only wild-type EHEC causes severe diarrhea and hemorrhagic colitis; the intimin-deficient mutant shows no attachment to host cells and no disease, while both EspA- and EspB-deficient mutants attach to host cells but do not cause disease. questions.question: A mouse model is used to study gut microbiota dynamics. Mice are divided into three groups: Group A receives broad-spectrum antibiotics for 7 days, Group B receives a narrow-spectrum antibiotic targeting only Gram-positive bacteria for 7 days, and Group C receives no antibiotics. After treatment, fecal samples are analyzed. Analysis of the local metabolome revealed a 70% decrease in short-chain fatty acid concentrations in Group A, while Groups B and C maintained baseline levels. Additionally, amino acid profiles remained unchanged across all groups. Group A shows a 90% reduction in total bacterial diversity, a 95% decrease in Bacteroidetes, and a 98% decrease in Firmicutes. Group B shows a 60% reduction in Firmicutes, no change in Bacteroidetes, and a 40% reduction in overall diversity. Immune response markers, such as IL-6 and TNF-α, were measured in all groups and showed a transient 20% increase following antibiotic treatment, returning to baseline within 48 hours. This modulation was independent of the observed changes in microbiota composition. Group C shows no significant changes. All groups are then challenged with oral Salmonella. Microbial diversity in the mouse gut typically ranges from 200 to 500 distinct operational taxonomic units (OTUs) under standard laboratory conditions. Previous studies have shown that shifts in microbial diversity can occur within 48 hours of antibiotic administration, even before major changes in host physiology are detected. Only Group A develops severe Salmonella infection, with high pathogen loads and systemic spread; Group B shows mild infection, and Group C resists colonization. Considering the effects of antibiotic spectrum and microbiota composition, which group is most likely to experience dysbiosis-associated loss of colonization resistance?

questions.question: A freshwater protozoan predator is introduced into a microcosm containing three bacterial populations: (A) Escherichia coli O157:H7 carrying stx genes on a lambdoid bacteriophage, (B) Shigella dysenteriae 1 lacking stx genes, and (C) E. coli K-12 with no prophages. Activation of the phage lytic cycle in E. coli O157:H7 typically results in a 2- to 5-fold increase in phage particle release within 24 hours. However, the overall bacterial density remains stable under nutrient-rich conditions. After 48 hours, population A shows 80% survival, population B 20% survival, and population C 10% survival. Escherichia coli populations, when exposed to protozoan predation, can increase expression of stress response genes by up to 2-fold, independent of toxin production. In microcosm experiments, E. coli typically maintains a baseline survival rate of 15% without antibiotic intervention. Addition of enrofloxacin to the microcosm increases population A's survival to 95%, but does not affect B or C. A plasmid derived from lambdoid bacteriophage was detected in 60% of E. coli O157:H7 cells after 48 hours, with copy number remaining stable regardless of protozoan presence. This plasmid is commonly used to study horizontal gene transfer in microcosm experiments. Shiga toxin is detected only in population A after enrofloxacin treatment. Which population is responsible for Shiga toxin-mediated defense against protozoan predation under antibiotic stress?
